speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: June the 29th, 1995. We're interviewing John Janek Armer. The interviewer is Dani Katz in Sydney, Australia, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Dani Katz, K-A-T-Z. Today's date is Thursday, June 29, 1995. I'm conducting today's interview with John Armer, also known as Janek Armer, in Sydney, Australia, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon John. On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I'd like to thank you for your time today. To begin today's interview, I'd like to ask a series of background questions. If you could tell us your name and spell your surname please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name in Australia, John Armer, A-R-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Janek, J-A-N-E-K, Armer, A-R-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you have any other first names? A Hebrew first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew first name was Jakob, Jakob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jakob.

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-K-O-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Were you known by any nicknames or family names in your younger days when you were at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 26 of nine, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Have you had any other birth dates at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have-- I had, you know-- should I share that this is not the right birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could just tell me if you have another birth date on passports or documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: On passport documents, 26 of nine, 1925. But my real birthday was 18th of nine, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 69 and 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And can you tell me the city and country you were born in, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was Krakow and the country was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Krakow, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow is K-R-A-K-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. To start off with, could you tell me a bit about your family, your parents and brothers and sisters? Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two brothers and one sister. And about my family, my family, my parents were Orthodox Jews living in Poland. And my father was a grain and flour merchant before the war. And my father and his father and his his father was born in Poland for the last three or four generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's first name was Israel, Israel Mordechai Armer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you said he was an Orthodox Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an Orthodox Jew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he belong to any particular group of orthodoxy in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did belong to Belzer Hasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were a very strict orthodox group, weren't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were strict orthodox, yes. But not so strict, you know. My father wasn't so strict. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the Orthodox Jewish upbringing you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I remember was I went to the-- to the Jewish school in the afternoon, which called Cheder. Cheder. And in the morning, I went to the Roman Catholic Polish School. So before the-- before-- before lunch, I used to learn Polish and all the other secular thing. In the afternoon, I used to learn Hebrew and the Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy going to Cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, yes, we like it very much. We played a little bit soccer. And we fight with the rabbi, but it was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you fought with the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always used to, you know, make jokes, you know. It wasn't so-- it was just like a Cheder, you know. Everybody did what they wanted. It wasn't so strict as in the Polish school in the morning. There was no discipline, in other words. No strict discipline.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Cheder part of the Belzer community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was, yes. And it was enjoyable because you learned-- they had a system where they-- they did not-- they did not press people, the pupils. They did not press them so much. So-- so they still-- at the end of the week, they still knew what they had learned from the beginning of the week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the Cheder? Did it have a particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't have anything, because it was just a Shteibel. Do you know what the Shteibel is? It was just a-- a room, you know. And plain furniture in it. And you're sitting next on the-- on the plain chairs, like a bank. And you learn. You learn between 2:00 and 6:00. Four hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of your teachers or any of the rabbis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a Haskel Faiwel. Faiwel. Was my teacher, and he was very good. He was my teacher right through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell Faiwel?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-A-I-W-E-L, Faiwel.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was one of your teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was one of my best teachers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can you tell me about your mother? What do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother did not come from such Orthodox Jews. My mother came originally from-- from-- from Poland, which-- which used to belong before the war to Germany. She come from, I can't remember the city. But anyway, she comes from a-- from a German Jews. So she-- she was not so strict orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your mother's first name and her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother first name was Amalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name before she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Rosenbaum, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother helped my father in the business, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who would look after you and your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a permanent maid. She was with us for 26 years. And she brought up all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would remember the name of the maid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Maria. A good Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about her second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I wouldn't remember. That I was not interested in those days. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about times with your-- what would you call her, your nanny or your maid, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always used to call her Maria, because she did everything. She-- she was in the-- in the household. She knew all the-- all the rituals in kosher. So we could rely on her. And she was good with the children. I wouldn't remember, because I was the youngest one, so I wouldn't remember whether she was good with the other three children. But all I know was that we'd had her for-- when I was born, she was already 26 years there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any pets? Any animals at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, who could afford pets in Poland? [LAUGHS] No. No pets, no telephone, no radio, no television. What else do you want to know? No cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you enjoy yourself? What would you do to have some fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did, as I told you, after the Cheder we used to play soccer. There was a very big place over there, next to there, and we used to play soccer. And then we went to the synagogue for the evening prayer. And on Saturday we went to the synagogue. And on Sunday, we went-- Sunday was I had to go to the Cheder all day, because the Polish school was only from Monday till Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Polish school have a name? Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember it. [INAUDIBLE] School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what years were you at that school? Would you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the primer. I was from the beginning till about sixth class. Sixth class, actually. I went through six classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the years? Would you be able to recall the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was the last, it was 1936 or '37, I don't know, but something like that. Because 1938, I had my bar mitzvah, so that's when I finished the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you would have to work out 13 years from '25. '38. '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no such a bar mitzvah like here in Australia. We-- my father took a bottle of wine and a-- a bit of a cake, and we went to the synagogue. And I had to put on the tefillin and everybody was drinking L'Chaim. And that's it. That was the whole-- whole bar mitzvah. And on Saturday, we're called to the Torah. That's all. That's all. That's the bar mitzvah. And the bar mitzvah was on the day when the bar mitzvah was, like middle of the week [INAUDIBLE]. But it was in the middle of the week. It was celebrated in the morning prayer in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about your brothers and sister? First of all, their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my first brother was Isaac, the-- the-- the oldest one. And the-- the-- the-- the younger-- the middle one was Henry, Henrick they called it. Well, my brother Henrick, he died in concentration camp after the war. And my brother died a few years ago here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Henrick died after the war or at the end of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a result of--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a result of the malnutrition and all the sickness. He survived the war, but he didn't survive the Holocaust, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went to-- it's at Krakow-- there was a-- they took my sister with my parents to the now what is-- to the concentration camp. Not concentration camp, but to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't know, but it could be either Treblinka or Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name? You didn't tell me her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Karola. Karola.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married and she had a child and a husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married-- Lesman. L-E-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her husband and child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband survived after the war-- till after the war-- because he wasn't-- she-- she was taken because of the child, but he stayed. And he married again and he live in Israel. But I believe he died. He would be now in his 80s. I have no contact with him anymore at this stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had with him contact till 1984--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --where I went to visit him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His first name was Wolf. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened to your parents during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you want to know what happened to them? They went to the gas chambers. That's what you want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened beforehand? Where they in any camps that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my parents were not in a camp. My care-- my parents were first in Krakow. Then we went to Wieliczka. And from Wieliczka, we went to the-- we had to go to the ghetto in Krakow. But my father and my mother was never in the concentration camp, never in the work-- they were straight taken away to the gas chambers from ghetto. Well, my father was 56 and my mother was a little bit younger then, that particular time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll move on to Wieliczka in a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, or do you remember, the address where you lived in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Nine, number nine, Rynek Podguorski, Podgorze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Rynek Podguorski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rynek, R-Y-N-E-K, Podguorski, P-O-D-G-U-O-R-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the suburb in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Podgorze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-D-G-O-R-Z-E. It was just-- opposite was the ghetto. Opposite ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did that become part of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It always remained outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: It always remained outside the ghetto. That's what helped me, because I had the neighbors and I could get some food from there and smuggle into the ghetto during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any single event or any other things that you remember that stand out in your mind up to 1938, 1939? During your time at home, growing up at home in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not very much. It was just a normal routine where we went to school, we went to the Cheder, and we-- we celebrated all the Jewish holidays like Pesach, Shvuot, and-- and what you call it-- Simchat Torah, and all the other things. But it was not-- not-- and every summer, I used to go for a month or six weeks, to the-- to the country. I used to go to the parents of-- of-- of Maria, because they had a farm outside. And that's it. That was the whole-- we used to go every year there. We used to go there with her, and we came back after the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would take you and your brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not-- no. She didn't take all of-- take me and my younger bro-- my older brother. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a-- we had a good time on the farm with the cows and the horses. You know, that sort of thing. That was before the war, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not very far from Krakow. It was about just like from here to Katoomba. How long-- how much is that? 70 kilometers. Your time at school, you said you went to a Polish Catholic school. Were there many Jewish boys and girls at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My class, there were only three. And that was they tolerate us, because the school was open on Saturday. It was from-- from Monday to Saturday. But-- but the teachers tolerate us because we didn't come on Saturday. I went to synagogue. And I used to have a few non-Jewish friends, which I always used to go to them and they told me what was-- what-- what we learned on Sunday-- on Saturday-- and I had to catch up. It was every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any difficulties growing up as a Jewish boy in a Polish Catholic school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only to the last two years, were there-- between '35, '36 were the anti-semitism was growing in Poland due to the Hitler in the moment. But up till then, I didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the streets, did you notice anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't notice nothing. It was-- it was a very short distance from home to the school, walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after '35, '36? You said things started to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- there used to be anti-semitism, and particularly my father felt it in the trade, because there was one minister, he used to say-- he got out and he said, his word was you should not hate the Jews. But what you should do, you should boycott them. In other words, don't buy off them. In other words, you understand what I mean? And that's what we felt. He said, don't hate them, don't prosecute them, just boycott them. And that went right through the community. And we did not-- could do so much trade as of course before those-- before those years. But we still survive, because the competition-- they still have to come, because we were the cheapest or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you describe to me what happened between '36 up to '39? How did things change for you and your family? Was it anything sudden, or was it all very gradual?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it changed sudden. Up to '39, we didn't-- hardship, we did not feel. There was plenty to eat, and we sleeped and everyth-- there's no problem. But the change suddenly, 1939, when the German move into Poland. In the first few days, the first seven days, they knew. They had a list. They knew that we are the merchants. So they came and they took everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? Who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German took away all the flour and the grain and all the produce. They took-- they came with a big truck and they took it away. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of Germans? Were they army Germans? Were they civilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Army Germans. Army Germans. They-- they-- they came with a piece of paper and they-- they want to take it. And we had to sign it that we get the money for it, which we never did. In other words, the business went flat. If you have no goods to sell, what-- how can you conduct a business?

speaker: interviewer
text: So up to September '39, even though things were already difficult, you still were able to have a bit of business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Little bit of business. We used to buy on the black market and sell on the black market, because the business was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about kosher food? Were you still able to eat? You were a religious family, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we did manage. We managed. We managed. Up to the ghetto, we managed. But I was-- I didn't go into it, because that wasn't my department. Department belonged to my father and Maria. But Maria left in 1940. 1940-- As soon the things get too hot, she left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean the apartment belonged to your father and Maria?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the apart--

speaker: interviewee
text: The department. The departm-- the kosher department, the food, sh-- they were looking after it. Not the apartment, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the department. The food area.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food area, that's right. So I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said, you-- did you see the Germans come and take the food? You saw it with your own eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw it, yes. They were not only satis-- they were not satisfied as they-- as they-- confiscated all the grain and the flour and even the salt and the sugar from the-- from the shop. But they went as far as went to the premises and they went to where we live to see whether we didn't hide anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these German soldiers again that came to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were German soldiers, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the first-- first week of-- first, second week during-- in the beginning of the war. They didn't give anybody a chance to sell it or to hide it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen to all the other Jewish shops and businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Happened right through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish reaction? What was your parents' or your father's reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: What could he do? He couldn't do very much. He-- if you would-- if you would resist them, they-- they had-- they had guns with them, you know. How could you resist it? You were lucky that they leave you alone. There was no resistance. You couldn't do-- you couldn't resist it. You couldn't demand anything. The only thing my father did, after the first raid, they left something. So what he did, we had a hiding place where-- behind the sh-- behind the premises somewhere, where it was a-- a room where you couldn't-- you couldn't get in. They-- nobody knew about the room. And that's where we hide a lot of food and produce for ourselves, not for selling purposes, you know. So we were- we were lucky that we could still-- there was no bread available, so we had flour. We baked the bread, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it go on like this for? This is September--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was September. It went on till 1940, the beginning of '41. That's when we went to Velichka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So just before we go to Velichka, so from September '39 to 1940, what would your father have done? Was he still able to carry out his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was carrying on very limited, limited. There was nothing for sale, but he still managed to buy something and to sell, because we had a lot of contacts. A lot of-- a lot of the Polish peasant were-- what you call it-- they had-- they used to buy from us on credit. And there was-- he had a lot of-- they had a lot of credit with us, which they didn't pay. So they gave us something, you know, to keep us going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't-- we could survive. We could survive, like I said, to the end of the war, if they wouldn't do anything. Because we could alwa-- being in the same place, you could always help yourself somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, we went to Velichka. And from Velichka, you could not carry all the things. So the only thing which we carried is the money. The money and gold and jewelry, which my father still had it. And that was converted in Velichka into the food. We lived from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Again, before we get to Velichka, I want to try and understand. How did people react between September '39 and 1940? What was the general reaction in Krakow, the Jewish reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish reaction was that-- that-- that it's not going to be good under the Germans. That-- that they-- all the time, they want something else. They were not satisfied only with confiscated your produce. Later they want furs because the-- the war started. They were preparing the war against Russia. So everybody had to give the furs. We're not allowed to have any furs. And you know, before the war, the Jewish people had a lot of furs. The families, the mother and the sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything has to be taken away. All the jewelry has to be-- everything has to-- you had to give it. But of course, we didn't. We give some of it, not-- not all of it. When did they start asking for this materials, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, straight, maybe a month or six weeks after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So already in October, November of '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They demanded things, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people cooperate?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as they could, you know, as much as they could. Where they didn't cooperate-- I mean, with the furs, they had to. They knew-- there was a big pile, you know, was a pile as maybe 10-- 10 story pile of furs where they collected. And then later they converted into for the soldiers. You're not allowed to have any furs. And it was a hard life. It wasn't as good as before, but nobody starved. Still I say, I still my-- I mean, in our family, we did not starve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people begin to talk about leaving Poland or leaving Krakow to look for somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where can you live? You can't live. It is all-- you can't-- you couldn't live from Krakow to the-- to Warsaw, you couldn't live. You had to have papers, permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: From who? Who did you need these papers from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the German authorities. You couldn't board a train. You couldn't board a train. My auntie was living about just like from here to Katoomba, [INAUDIBLE], which is not far from Auschwitz. You could not board a train without the permission. The German soldier were on the station. And you have to have a-- a pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they introduced the-- the, what you call it, in the first few months they introduce it. You had to have, what you call-- what is it called?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like an armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armband, yes, with the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Already in September, October?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. A little bit later, little bit later.

speaker: interviewer
text: November?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yes. You had to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what color was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: White with a blue-- blue Magen David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Star of David, yes. And you could not-- if they catch you without it, you would be in a big trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said papers. What sort of papers did you have to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we're in Krakow, you didn't have to have papers when you were in this surrounding. But you couldn't move. You say you leave Poland. How can you leave Poland? You can't live anywhere. And all the borders were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was like this throughout 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything happen in 1940 in Krakow? Any significant events to the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were talking about building the ghetto. They did eventually, you know. And that was the last thing was they did it. Because-- because before they build the ghetto, the Jewish people used to live everywhere in Krakow. In [? Platz, ?] in Padgorze, anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they build the-- the ghetto. But the ghettos, they built later. I think 1941 they were built, because we were first in Velichka. Were there any people, any individuals, in charge of the Jewish population that you know of in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was Judenrat, they called it. Jewish, there was the-- like a Jewish wel-- not welfare. You call it here welfare, but over there it was Judenrat. That was the president--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish council. A Jewish council.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. President and all the other thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know the names of any people that were on that Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would not remember. All I remember is was one of them was Simcha Spira. Simcha Spira-- how do you spell it? S-I-M-C-H-A, Spira, S-P-I-R-A. He was the-- he was the president of the ghetto. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later on. But before that, I can't remember. There was one-- oh yes. There was one by the name of Rubinstein, R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N. He was, before the war, a teacher. And he was, I think, president or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from the beginning. They formed the Judenrat straightaway, as soon as the German came in. Because they could not go to individual Jew what they wanted. They went to the Judenrat and the Judenrat went to the Jews and demanded what-- whatever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: This Rubinstein, do you remember maybe what his first name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So what-- can you explain to me what was the-- the channel of command? If the Germans wanted something, what did they do? You said they didn't go to the individual Jews. What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they went to the Judenrat, and they tell them that they want that, and that day all the Jews should wear the armband. So the Judenrat went and he put up-- put up all the sign. And-- and announce it, and that's it. There was not a radio announce or television. It was just they had paper, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Posters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Posted on every corner, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else would the-- the Germans ask the Judenrat to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's where they demand the furs, so they did it the same way. They posted and everybody had to go get. Or they want jewelry. Right from the beginning, the first, they said anybody who would be find with a weapon would be shot on the spot. That was right-- I've forgotten to tell you-- that's right from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first it was they take care of the weapon. And then they take care of the food. Then they took care of the furs. Then they took care of the jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Systematically, they make the Jews poorer and poorer. And then when they make them poor, then they shift them from one place to another till they brought them to ghetto. And then they exterminate them from the ghetto to there-- it was a-- it was a organized, systematical-- systematic extermination, put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said weapons. Would many Jews have had weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not if I know, but-- nobody had-- not-- not-- not what I know had anybody weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents, your family, wouldn't have had weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no way. They wouldn't jeopardize the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but even before the Germans came.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody had a weapon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said before Velichka, what-- what happened that led up to Velichka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left Krakow and we went to Velichka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who's we, you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. We didn't go because we-- we went out on the [? cart, ?] because my-- my-- my father's brother, with the children, were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of your father-- he's your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the name of your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Jakob Armer. He was a younger brother. He had 11 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what, your father decided it was getting too difficult in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went there because this uncle had only one son. And when the German came into Velichka, right from the beginning, the first few weeks, they rounded up 50 Jews. And between the 50 Jews was my uncle and his older son. And they took them outside Velichka and they shot them for no reason. So there was just the auntie with the children left and a business to run. So my father, we went over there together, you know, to help them as much as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with your mother, your father, your two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my younger brother, because my older brother was somewhere else. And my sister, her husband, and a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Velichka when, 19-- end of 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or early 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I wouldn't know because-- when was the liquid-- the liquida-- before the liquidation of the ghetto, we came back. Would be end of-- we were there approximately 12 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any-- in Velichka you said your father went to work or help in his brother's business.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to-- he went to his brother's-- his widow-- the brothers' wife business. But there was not enough business over there. So he used to come-- he used to travel every day from Velichka to Krakow to look after his business, because we still have the business over there. We didn't live over there, but the business was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you lived in Velichka for that period.

speaker: interviewee
text: We live Velichka.

speaker: interviewer
text: At some--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to-- And he used to go in the morning, come back in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to make some business over there. He used to get some fruit and some other things from the peasant and used to sell it. And some of them he was to brought back to-- to Krakow-- to Velichka.

speaker: interviewer
text: But basically, you lived like free people in Velichka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we lived like free people.

speaker: interviewer
text: At some stage I understand--

speaker: interviewee
text: We even attend to the synagogue. There was a synagogue over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was still allowed to open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, still open, yes, still allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the synagogues in Krakow? Were they still allowed to be open in 1940, '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was open, but you had to be careful when you go in. You had to be careful in Velichka also. They didn't know where the synagogue-- synagogue was somewhere, you know, on the side street where nobody knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: At some time, Velichka, I understand, became a-- like a concentration point where they brought Jews from different areas. Do you know anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know anything. I left Velichka a long time ago. I left Velichka before the liquidation of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now I'm just trying to clarify that, if you-- if you remember at any time, Jews from other suburbs or provinces being brought into Velichka.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't remember it. Because we went-- we went like a freelance. We were now on a [? cart, ?] you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. No, but I understand that Jews were taken from Krakow to Velichka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. But not-- we went-- we went early. Then we went back from Velichka to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Veli--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know from another interview--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, That's the end of tape one of the interview with John Armer. It's the 29th of June, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. [INAUDIBLE]
